elements.sent-2411-001: We explore the behaviour emerging from learning agents repeatedly interacting strategically for a wide range of learning dynamics that includes projected gradient, replicator and log-barrier dynamics.

elements.sent-2411-003: We obtain a Folk Theorem-like result and characterise the set of payoff vectors that can be obtained by these dynamics, discovering a wide range of possibilities for the emergence of algorithmic collusion.
elements.sent-2411-004: Recent advancements in Machine Learning and Artificial Intelligence have driven the widespread adoption of learning algorithms across many domains, such as pricing, auctions, and advertising.
elements.sent-2411-005: However, there is a growing literature to show that these algorithms may learn to collude without explicit coordination or instruction, which presents significant challenges, both economic and regulatory (see \cite{ezrachi2016virtual , \cite{gautier2020ai , \cite{cartea2022algorithms ,\cite{hartline2024regulation and references therein).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-006: The potential for collusion among learning agents was demonstrated in \cite{calvano2020artificial through a pricing game, where agents employing learning algorithms consistently selected prices above competitive levels.}
elements.sent-2411-007: Notably, when one agent deviated to competitive pricing, others would temporarily transition to competitive prices as well, before reverting to the original above competitive pricing after a period of time.
elements.sent-2411-008: This pattern of alternating between cooperative and punitive phases mirrors equilibrium strategies extensively studied in the context of repeated games.
elements.sent-2411-009: According to the Folk Theorem in repeated games, such strategies can sustain a wide range of payoff vectors, including those associated with collusion, provided the agents are sufficiently patient.
elements.sent-2411-010: This result highlights the theoretical underpinnings of collusive behaviour and its practical implications for algorithmic interactions in strategic environments.
elements.sent-2411-011: This paper demonstrates that outcomes similar to those observed in \cite{calvano2020artificial are not isolated phenomena.}
elements.sent-2411-012: We focus on $q$-replicator dynamics \cite{sakos2022convergence , a class of learning dynamics that generalises replicator dynamics, projected gradient dynamics, and log-barrier dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-013: By leveraging classical tools from the repeated games literature, we analyse the set of strategy profiles and their corresponding payoff vectors that can emerge under these dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-014: For games with complete information and perfect monitoring, we establish a Folk Theorem (see, \cite{aumann1994long , \cite{rubinstein1994equilibrium , and \cite{fudenberg2009folk ): any feasible and individually rational payoff vector can be learned through $q$-replicator dynamics.}}}

elements.sent-2411-017: By transitioning from specific instances of algorithmic collusion to a more general setting, we gain a broader understanding of collusion as an emergent outcome of repeated interactions in learning environments.
elements.sent-2411-018: Similarly, by moving from multi-agent learning to the solution concepts of game theory, we obtain a fresh perspective on these concepts, many of which naturally align with and find new relevance within a learning framework.
elements.sent-2411-019: We study a setting where players simultaneously apply Reinforcement Learning (RL) algorithms to optimise their strategies in a repeated game.
elements.sent-2411-020: Each player iteratively updates their strategy based on their learning algorithm to improve their reward.
elements.sent-2411-021: Since the game involves repeated interactions, players can condition their actions on the recent history of play.
elements.sent-2411-022: Our framework accommodates various monitoring structures -- perfect, public, and private -- and applies to both complete and incomplete information settings.
elements.sent-2411-023: When the players apply learning algorithms, a dynamical system is induced in each agent’s space of strategies.
elements.sent-2411-024: Analysing these dynamics is a formidable challenge, as each agent's decisions directly influence the learning trajectories of the other agents by shaping the environment in which they are playing.
elements.sent-2411-025: However, under certain conditions, these dynamics converge to a Nash equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-026: Specifically, some Nash equilibria are ``attracting'', meaning that if the initial strategy profile lies within a sufficiently small neighbourhood of such an equilibrium, the dynamics converge to it.
elements.sent-2411-027: We characterise the set of payoff vectors that can be learned (i.e. are attracting from a non-zero measure of initial conditions) and obtain a Folk Theorem result.
elements.sent-2411-028: Under perfect monitoring, the set of learnable payoff vectors coincides with the set of feasible and individually rational payoff vectors.
elements.sent-2411-029: For models with imperfect monitoring, we provide a characterisation of the strategy profiles that can be learned, highlighting the role of monitoring structures in shaping the outcomes of multi-agent learning.

elements.sent-2411-230: Our next results build upon these theorems by combining them with the Folk Theorem for repeated games.
elements.sent-2411-231: The first important implication is that, in a reinforcement learning framework, the richness of the set of equilibrium payoffs that the Folk Theorem guarantees can be recovered with sufficiently long recall.

elements.sent-2411-233: The study of repeated games often focuses on the correspondence between Nash equilibria and the payoff vectors they generate.
elements.sent-2411-234: One aspect of this relationship is given by the celebrated Folk Theorem.
elements.sent-2411-235: Two important assumptions of the Folk Theorem are perfect monitoring, which is that each player's signal precisely identifies the action profile played in each period, and that the players have unbounded recall.
elements.sent-2411-236: Under these assumptions, the Folk Theorem characterises the set of payoff vectors that correspond to Nash equilibria as the set of feasible and individually rational payoffs, shortly to be defined.
elements.sent-2411-237: However, \cite{barlo2016bounded establish that this set of payoff vectors can be approximated by equilibrium payoff vectors when players are restricted to have finite recall.}
elements.sent-2411-238: This work establishes a crucial connection between the Folk Theorem and our main results.

elements.sent-2411-244: Any payoff vector where each player’s payoff is at least their minmax value is termed individually rational, as each player's payoff is at least what they can obtain unilaterally.
elements.sent-2411-245: A feasible payoff vector is a payoff vector that can be obtained as the expected reward of a mixed strategy.

elements.sent-2411-258: In the case of public monitoring, the signal is publicly observable and identical for all players.

elements.sent-2411-633: Throughout this paper, we have been considering mixed strategies where the strategy of player $i \in N$ is a distribution over pure strategy profiles, which play deterministically after each private history.

elements.sent-2411-635: In the following, we consider behavioural strategies for a perfect monitoring structure where, in each period of the repeated game, every player observes all actions and payoffs from that period.
